speaker: interviewer
text: It's rolling. Please, [?Kitia?]. So you blackmailed your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were taken, and we found out-- my parents and myself found ourselves outside the ghetto, working in the shop. And gradually there were about six or eight hundred people left that either were, at the time of the aktion, in hiding or who came from the ghetto at a later stage or whom Rosner claimed. Rosner was sending people on the pretense of bringing clothing for the-- from the ghetto. It was the intention to put under the clothing as many people as possible, and you have about six to eight hundred people who were left. And gradually it was in the times, the number shrank to 50, 25 men, 25 women.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family was in that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was among them. My girlfriend who is in Sweden was among them. The third one ran away with help of Rosner. And in the meantime Rosner was arrested.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents were, a week or so later, sent to a labor camp in Annaburg. And there was a selection and Rosner called us and assured us that our parents are going to a labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say a week or so later you're talking about the 7th or so, of August '43.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, perhaps it was the 15th or perhaps it was the 1st of September.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember exactly. Time was not measured--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not of the essence.

speaker: interviewee
text: --by hours or by minutes, only events. Sometimes in an hour things happen that don't happen in one's lifetime. So I approached one of the SS men who was conducting-- I remember his name, Ludwig. I saw him very often in the shop. He was conducting that selection. And I approached him and I said that my mother is a very good housefrau, and a very clean housewife, and perhaps, if it will be convenient, she could work in the kitchen. And indeed, she worked in the kitchen until she was replaced by somebody younger, by another commandant, and so on and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I stayed in Bedzin till after June '44. And I remember that because our contact with the outside world became very limited, and the only contact-- official contact-- was when we were allowed take the rubbish out to a rubbish dump outside the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you manage to take the rubbish out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had to be escorted that by the guards, push the barrel [POINTS] with the rubbish in the middle of the roads, guards on two sides of the barrel, and we went to the rubbish dump. And over there was Polish workers, and they approached me and they said, the invasion has started. I didn't know what the invasion is. I was a completely apolitical animal. I came back, and I told them the wonderful news. Look, I've got a limited time. I can't dwell on any other episode, which important as it might be and significant as it is, can't fit in to one hour. So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it must have been soon after the invasion, triggered off by escape of two children which I have also arranged. Anyhow, we were sent out--

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you rescued your niece. Was this the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My niece-- my cousin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh your cousin, I beg your pardon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my cousin I put in care of a Polish woman. 10 days or a week before the Judenrein. And her parents would have been alive, perhaps, if they wouldn't have gone, on Saturday, home to do some washing and so on. My-- not only my aunt, but generally, parents were very, very reluctant to part with the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Although as you and many other interviewers cannot comprehend how come that they still did not know what's happening? We could not afford to absorb the truth. We just could not. That would have been a mass suicide. So we knew that there is a fight going on, a fight for survival. And each and every one of us thought that he would be on the winning side. Namely, the day-- the time is on his or her side. And the general motto was, whatever will happen to me, it will happen to my child. As far as young adults are concerned, like me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my parents, their situation was much more painful because the parents were aware, as much as we were, that youth has a better chance of survival. Never mind the criteria, but one of them is youth, young and strong. And-- I had fantastic stories with Rosner with offers of escape. And one day I came home-- and also, before all that-- because of that, the social structure of the family underwent a radical and very painful change. It wasn't any more the authority of the father, as it used to be throughout generations and generations. The person who put the bread on the table was the person who was in the seat of authority. And the bread was put by the youth. The young people were employed. My employment covered my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So even on the level of daily life, my mother referred to me more than to my father. The blow to his-- Ego.

speaker: interviewee
text: --ego, to his dignity, was devastating, absolutely devastating. And the tension between us grew. I resented that because of me, he suffers. He resented that I, maybe unwillingly, have got the authority. I was in contact with the events, not him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a very painful situation. One day I came and I said that I've got a possibility to run away, to have forged papers, and so on. And I said that half jokingly, I mean, today this possibility presented-- at least to me, it was completely unrealistic because in the end, the person who was supposed to provide me with the false paper bring me to Vienna, I ask him, [SPEAKING GERMAN], and what will happen in Vienna? And he said to me, in Vienna, my Fraulein, I leave you to your own intelligence. And I said, to my intelligence? I am not going. And that part of this I repeated to my parents. My father turned away, and I saw that his shoulders, you know, are sagging. Hunched.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother got tears in her eyes, and she said to me, we know, my darling, that you have got the right to survive. But you have to know that the moment you leave us, you sign our death sentence. But by that time I refused to go already. I refused to go. But that put a completely different perspective on my role in their survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: It gave you a burden and a legacy.

speaker: interviewee
text: I might have been protecting them, but I was completely unaware of the depth and of the significance of my protection. I might have seen myself as the one who brings the extra portion of bread, but I never saw myself as the one who decides about their life and death. But you see, I-- at- by that time I already refused to go. And my mother has survived. And many other people suffered from a sense of guilt after the war. I never had that sense of guilt. Not because I didn't go, because I could also twist the events around and show myself in the light of a semi-saint. I did refuse to go because I felt insecure. That's why I refused to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father perished 10 days before the end of the war, because he was such a believer in German integrity that all-- he was on a death march. And people around him said, go, let's run away and save myself-- save ourselves. Not him. He said, they told us to march. If they find us, they will kill us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the middle of winter he marched, and he felt-- he fell. And either he was left there to freeze to death, or a German bullet finished him off. And that was, in this part of Poland, it was about 10 days before the end of the war. So when did that place, what date-- not what date exactly, but what month?

speaker: interviewee
text: I reconstructed that it could have been in February '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that part of Poland was already freed but the Russians in February. So therefore-- so, you see, therefore this also is very important for all our testimonies. I can say that I probably have forgotten a lot of things. I probably cannot address some of my memories. But I also am sure that I don't edit any of my memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is a very important fact.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which is a very important thing. I might choose not-- perhaps not I might choose, but it has chosen not to be dealt with yet. I cannot address my stay in Auschwitz. My Auschwitz experience is on a-- I came to Auschwitz after Annaburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we just go back to-- you made the decision not to leave your parents, and what happened from there? Where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I told you, there was the Judenrein and eventually in '44 I went to that labor camp Annaburg. And from Annaburg-

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Annaburg during the attempt on Hitler's life, which was in July-- 20th of July I think, '44. Which means I wasn't there very long. And sometime in August, I think, the labor camp was liquidated and the majority were sent to Auschwitz. And I was among the people who were sent to Auschwitz. The Auschwitz experience belongs to a totally different sphere of experiences. To me it is so bizarre and so different from the previous-- already previous-- war and persecution experiences, that I cannot address it as-- how should I say, I cannot address it as freely. My arrival to Auschwitz was an end of a certain period in my life forever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it the end--

speaker: interviewee
text: The arrival in Auschwitz was a beginning of an experience that stayed with me also forever. It was not an experience that could have been-- that can, in my opinion, be put on par with any other experiences during the Holocaust. Being in the way of, of violence, of torture, of-- perhaps brutalization and dehumanization are the only two words that apply to Auschwitz. The brutalization was more spiritual and emotional than physical. Nothing could be more physical than a stay in Auschwitz. The length of stay in Auschwitz, I don't think it's important. In my case I think I came in August, and I was in Auschwitz during the uprising which, now we all know, took place on the 8th of October. So I could have been between six weeks to two months in Auschwitz. It's the intensity of the experience that marks you, not the duration of the experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that intensity--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the intensity occurs immediately. Immediately we were shocked and we stayed in a state of shock.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many of you arrived at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell you. There were thousands and thousands in our cattle truck were perhaps 100. You never related to the other people, only to the one with whom you were close, and that's it. And among the one with whom you were close, if you had one or two, you were very lucky. Because Auschwitz was the most isolated place in the whole world. It was swarming with hundreds of thousands of nameless and faceless and shapeless creatures. Nobody knew who you are. Nobody spoke your language. Nobody knew you from before. Nobody knew your past.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, when you arrived there were you with that friend of yours that you worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The two of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived, we arrived to Auschwitz as human beings. All right, we were in camp. But we were washed, we were cleaned, we wore our own clothing, perhaps not the pre-war, but the blouse that was handed for- to me was, from the moment it was handed, it was mine. We ate food that was cooked for us. We sat at the tables. We had the bowl. We had the spoon. The dishes were washed. The bed sheets were sheets.

speaker: interviewer
text: You did all human things-- all normal human things.

speaker: interviewee
text: Human things. On the lower level, deprived, limited, but normal human things. And the shock of totally bizarre experience, unknown, unprecedented. Even if we heard rumors that there is Auschwitz, Auschwitz was a name. Nobody could describe it, because the one who could describe it, the one who had the ultimate experience, are not here to tell the tale.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stepped out and I thought it was dawn because there was a glow on the sky. It was a deathly silence. And you'll find it in many testimonies about Auschwitz that when a transport arrived at night or towards daybreak, it was silent. It was quiet. It was such a deafening silence. Not the bark of a dog, not a scream, not to shout, not a cry, not a moan, nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: As if there were never any people there on that platform. There were thousands of people. We were stunned. We were paralyzed. And we turned around, and it wasn't a glow. It was a fire from four chimneys of crematoria. There was no glow. And we were a tight group of three or four girls, and I remember I turned to them and I said to them, girls, it's all true.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then everything you heard formalized in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then we knew that what we heard must have been true. Even if it was on an intellectual, rational, and human level rejected, we were aware that such information was planted. It was rejected by our mind. Our minds were not depraved yet. They were only starved, a little starved of stimulations. Although my word, this life offered us plenty of stimulation, but not of intellectual stimulation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what--

speaker: interviewee
text: But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we definitely rejected that. I remember there was a men whom I was told was whom I went to school together. He was two years ahead of me. And they told me that he escaped from Auschwitz and that this and this time he will pass through the window, and I can see him.

speaker: interviewer
text: I are really stood at the window and I saw Shimek Lewinstein, and I said, how can you believe what he's says? Look at his eyes. They are the eyes of a madman. And that's what it was. And the fact that the people who survived Auschwitz are not mad are only due to the kindness of nature. Nature gave us a huge anesthetic. Healing.

speaker: interviewee
text: That started to wear off only 50 years after, a huge anesthetic. We didn't feel the cold. We didn't feel the hunger because by that time hunger was state of mind. We were deprived of all human emotions. We didn't look human. We didn't feel human. And we didn't think human.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just told you.

speaker: interviewer
text: August '44. And did you do any work in that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the work was device, like everything else in Auschwitz, to demean you, to deprive you of their last vestige of humanity, although it was none of them left. Auschwitz was just more than work or torture. Auschwitz was the-- how shall I tell you? Auschwitz was the place where you were naked-- emotionally naked, spiritually naked, and of course physically naked. You came to that place. You knew that you won't get out of it. You didn't have to pretend.

speaker: interviewer
text: You knew you won't get out of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, there were the four chimneys. The informations in '44 told us that Jews are being exterminated. And here you come to a place that has got four glowing chimneys, day and night. The air is putrid with a smell that you cannot identify. Who ever of us smelt a smell of burning human flesh? You don't look human anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you tattooed? Did you receive a number in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have got a tattoo. Do you want to see it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could we see your number please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes I was among the one--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your number.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to look it up. That's also choice of my memory that I elect. It's A25441. My girlfriend who survived has got her last digit O. She was before me. She has A2544O. I was behind her, 441.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you were numb. You were completely numb. If I recall something from Auschwitz, my only concern was to retrieve my cloak from the mud and put one foot in front off the other. We had to be on the move. We were in quarantine, [? alright?]. We were on a quarantine. We were housed, lodged, collected, lived. There is not an adjective or a word in a human language, no matter whether its English, or Spanish, or whatever who could describe the conditions in Auschwitz because they were not human conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I bring you forward a little?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, how I got out of Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, how you got out.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got out of Auschwitz because my girlfriend, obviously, was not as deeply shocked as I was. I was shocked into a state of a complete inertia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anatomic.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said-- you see when we were entering Auschwitz through that infamous gate-- Arbeit macht frei-- I was on the left side, on the edge of the left side. And at the gate stood a guard, a German guard. And they all urged you to go quick, quick, fast, fast, schnell, schnell, [GERMAN], and the shrieks, and the tempo, and the reality of the whole thing. And this guard was standing, and as I was passing he said to me in German, to Auschwitz you enter through the gate and out you go. And he jerked his head towards the smoke. And then we were running, and we were running. And I really thought that we are in an asylum, in a huge asylum, that they put us in an asylum. Anyhow, at one stage they-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then something very unusual happened. Eventually from all big groups, small groups emerge. In every big group, you don't stay in a big group, you- you form a tighter group.

speaker: interviewer
text: You break up.

speaker: interviewee
text: A smaller group. And the people with whom we were in Annaburg, we kept together, few people. And suddenly a German in a striped uniform with a red triangle-- I didn't know what that means, none of it-- approached me. I think we were perhaps ten girls. As I said we still looked very human. And he addressed me in a beautiful, elegant German. And I answered him in an equally elegant and correct and educated German. And he engaged me in a conversation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, is there anything that you would like to know? And the girls started to nudge me, ask him if it's true. And I asked him, is it true? He didn't-- I didn't qualify my question, and he didn't ask me to qualify my question. He said, Fraulein, we cannot answer your-- all your questions. Is there anything else that you want to know? So they asked him-- they said, ask him if our head will be shorn, because we have seen people that-- suddenly all the informations that we were gathering came to the foreground. And I asked him, he said, no, your hair will be cut short. Ask him-- you know, I don't remember. That was the sort of answers- questions that I was asking him. And then I ask him, was ist Auschwitz? What is Auschwitz? And he gave me an answer that I hope I will never forget. And he said to me, Fraulein, whatever is normal in the world outside Auschwitz cannot happen in Auschwitz. And whatever is normal in Auschwitz will never happen in the world outside Auschwitz. And I asked him if he is a Jew. And he said, no. I said so-- he had the beautifully tailored striped uniform. And he said, you see that number? [POINTS] He said, I was a lawyer in Vienna. I have forgotten my telephone number from my office, but this number [POINTS] I will never forget.

speaker: interviewee
text: He snapped his fingers, I had a feeling that I am in some elegant cafe house. And a boy, I think, appeared, everything in stripes, and he said something to him. An he brought a bucket of steaming hot tea with a ladle. And he said [GERMAN], and we didn't want to drink. You're speaking about power of life. We didn't want to drink. It could have been poisoned. On the threshold of gas chamber, such was the disbelief that we can die. We couldn't accept that, and he understood that, and he took the first ladle. It was tea. Sweet, real tea that we haven't tasted that for five years. Then he snapped his fingers, and the boy appeared with sandwiches. None of us dreamt to eat the sandwiches. We only peeped inside. My sandwich was sardines with honey. I licked a bit of honey and hid the sandwich. I never ate it. Because we- I pushed it somewhere in my shoes, and we had to go to-- we went to a shower. I never ate it. But I thought to myself, it wasn't- the combination, even, wasn't strange.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who was this person? He said, remember my name. My name is Dieter. Remember my number, number of course I don't remember. I never related to numbers. If you ever will need something, tell me. I said, how will I find? He said, I will find you. The next time I saw him it must there be a few hours later. We were thrown into a room which was-- which was, fortunately, a shower. No towels, no soap, naked, with our body hair shorn, and pushed to run and run forwards through a space that was on both sides had a barbed wire. And on one side of the barbed wire men were standing. And he was among them. And all the sophistication, and all the Austrian culture all disappeared from his face. It was the face of a lecherous man, licking his fingers-- licking his lips, looking at the parade of young, naked, female bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh my dear.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stopped just for a fraction of a second. And I said to him, are you satisfied? And I don't know where from, I swear to you, I never heard the word before, [SPEAKING GERMAN] It's the process of dehumanization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he answer you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I said to him. [SPEAKING GERMAN] That was the last I saw of him. And there were people I met. One girl from my town, and the first message that I got was never to have anything to do with him. Apparently he was a- a kapo, and he was dealing with diamonds, whatever it was. Now Auschwitz, the peak of inhumanity was also a source of the greatest and deepest humanity. If there was a humanity there was in Auschwitz. The fact that somebody addressed you.

speaker: interviewer
text: We came to a barrack. I was given a bowl, an enamel, chipped, red bowl that had a hole. And my first instinct was to tip the bowl towards me so the liquid won't run out through the hole. Later on I found out what the hole was for. And I looked around and I said, anybody has got a spoon? And there was a woman standing, to me she was old, but by that time I didn't know how I look anymore. And she said to me in Yiddish, eat my child, eat, that's all what you'll ever get.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I put my lips to that bowl, at first my stomach rumbled. And I looked around, and everybody was lapping up like dogs, like animals. And so did I. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I mean that was an act of humanity-- a stranger. A stranger that helped you to stand up. A stranger that moved close to you to give you a bit of warmth. A stranger that you never remembered his face. A person that you saw today and she disappeared. A person who could help you to get up from the mud. If you drop your portion of bread that even there was someone who helped you to pick it up. There were others who stole it, but everybody survived by what she thought helps her to survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: One by stealing, one by murdering, and one by remaining humane. OK.
